speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Myra Slotnick, and I'm interviewing Edith Klar in Las Vegas, Nevada, on June the 7th, 1996. And this is tape number 2. Edith, tell me the circumstances under which your mother took you to the convent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother had heard about the, a convent in the outskirts of Brussels. And this convent housed Jewish children and protected them from the Germans, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, I do not remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the name of that convent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother contacted the nuns there. And arrangements were made for me to be taken there. It was a devastating experience for me. The news that I had to go to a place where I would not have my parents with me, where I would be alone [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was more than I could handle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cried my heart out. My mother tried to explain to me, but I didn't understand it. Why are you doing this to me? What did I do? Whatever I did, I won't do it again. Please, please, pleading, pleading, don't put me there. They had to. They took me to the convent one morning. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the time of the year? What was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was winter. I, I'm not-- I really-- I think it was the fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing? What were you wearing when you went to the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wearing, I think, a pretty outfit. I don't remember exactly. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing a coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I think a white dress. And I think I was wearing a coat. And I went in there, and of course, the nuns were wonderful. For them to do this was a great sacrifice. And of course, I realized that years later. For them to do something like that-- I went in there, and I remember talking to the nun. I remember her facing me. And my mother and father were there. And I just didn't know what was going to happen to me. I was asked to go into a room and change into the garb, the Catholic garb, put a cross around my neck, and I came out dressed that way. My parents had left, and I remember a lot of prayer. I had to pray. And I had to do the cross all day. That's all I remember. I remember no interaction with anybody. I remember nothing around me as far as any kind of contact. I was alone. I was so unhappy, all I did was cry. I just cried and cried. And I remember echoing hallways, echoing constantly. People walking, just like in the movies. Exactly like in films when you see a convent, quiet, silence, prayer.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so strange to me. I was brought up so Jewish. And this was so foreign for me. Where was my mother? How did your family practice Judaism in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: They observed whatever they could with whatever they had, which was so little. There was really nothing there. But they kept the holidays and-- quietly in the darkness. It was always dark. There was no daylight. I never saw daylight, very rarely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any other children in the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember seeing children, but somehow, there was no interaction. It was a cold feeling. It wasn't done purposely. I think it was just life at that time, in that period of, of, of time, in that era, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your duties at the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just prayer and prayer. And I think there was some schooling. I don't remember really well. But my mother came to visit me every couple of weeks. And I would just be hysterical, absolutely hysterical crying. I said, please take me home. Although home was misery, to me, it was heaven. I didn't-- to me, that was wonderful, being with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your parents when you were in the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were on Rue Picard, the place where I left, where they lived before in hiding. And my mother fought with my father continuously. They had words about my mother wanted to take me home. My father said no, let her live. She'll survive. You know we'll die any minute. It was a moment-to-moment fear that somebody would come to that door and take them away. And he said no. And she begged him. She had an increasing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] foreboding, a terrible feeling about the convent. Each time she came to visit me, it accelerated. She felt a premonition of some sort. She told my dad, I just don't feel good about this place. I feel something's going to happen to [? Edith. ?] They called me [? Edith ?] in German. And my father said no. He was adamant. Because he said, you know, we can't do this. We can't afford to take her out. But my cries were so pathetic. And I remember just being devastated. And I, I hated it, just hated the place. I had nothing. Nobody talked to me. I don't remember anybody talking to me. It was a cold, cold, miserable atmosphere. One Sunday night, my mother came with my dad again. And I was out of control. And my mother said, you know, I don't care. I'm taking her home. There's something is going to happen here. I feel it. My mother is very intuitive. So am I, to this day. And she really feels things. And this was an apprehension that she had.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- they took me out. Said this has got to be-- and my dad agreed. He started to feel the same thing. They took me home. I was so ecstatic. I mean, this was living, you know? No interaction with children, nothing, but this was living. I was happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: No food. At least I had food in the convent. That was something there. But that didn't mean anything to me. That was not the priority. [SIGHS] The next morning, a neighbor came to the door on Rue Picard and told my mother that she had heard and had read in the paper that 60 Jewish children were taken out of the, of that convent in the middle of the night. And they took the nuns with them. And come to find out that later they were sent to a death camp and all exterminated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said, oh, my god, my child. I was minutes away, hours away from losing my child. She said, you see, she said to my dad. You see? I felt it. You've got to listen to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life wasn't any better for any of them. It just-- it grew progressively worse. And there was no future. There was no hope for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I believe, was 1944, possibly, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long, actually, were you in the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably was there a couple of months. It seemed years. But I think I was just there a couple of months. But the memory is so vivid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me now-- you went back to see your-- you went back to live with your parents. Tell me about your life after that and prior to liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just my life prior to liberation? My father made it a point, seriously, to tutor me, to make sure that if I did ever have the opportunity to go to school, that I could at least blend in and not have such a difficult time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just longed to go to school. I'd heard of people going to school and learning. And, and I wanted to play, you know? And I never-- I, I couldn't go out. And that bothered me that I couldn't go out. I was always restrained.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to this day, these memories keep coming, but I don't think a day goes by that I don't remember my life as it went. It's like maybe lest I forget that I was spared, and all these other poor people, millions of them died. And I think that there is something there that stops me from forgetting. And maybe it's, it's something special for these people. And I feel it. I have such tremendous compassion for what happened to so many and so many of my relatives. And it's really--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you read by that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I could. My father was very good in tutoring. He was self-tutored also. His life was never an easy life. None of theirs was. And he was very knowledgeable. And he was able to do this. He taught me math. And he taught me, you know, everything in French, although at home, I conversed in German. And also I knew the French. And I, I was bilingual then. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it just doesn't leave you. And I've actually been trying for years and years to get-- I feel like I'm still trying to get well. And I feel I'm making a lot of progress. But it's my whole makeup has suffered, I think. I get periods of sadness that I feel. And I know it goes back to my life as it was-- and sometimes difficult. I have a great time. But sometimes difficult-- something stops me, sometimes, from enjoying my life. It's like a barrier to be broken through. And I've tried to treat myself, therapy, therapeutically, to try to get rid of a lot of this. And I think my parents have suffered from it more than I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me-- your father tutored you. And then how long did that go on before your life changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: That went on for a couple of years. And one day, which was just an absolute miracle, we heard singing in the streets, shouting, singing, happiness. And we all thought what is this? We didn't really have newspapers. We didn't have phones. We had no contact with the world. I was very sheltered. And I heard yelling and screaming in a nice way. No soldiers, no boots. I went to sleep with this every night and woke up 100 times a night with those horrible boots that I heard marching. I hear them in my dreams today.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said, we're free. Do you know that we're free? I didn't understand, even. What it is that? You can play now. You can go to school. I mean, there was such jubilation. It was, it was incredible, the, the energy, the-- But I didn't quite-- I still had trouble understanding-- what? Where are they? Where did the German soldiers go? What happened to them? Are they still across the street? Well, we open the door. We open the door? We're going outside? I mean, this was like-- I didn't know whether to have fear, to be happy. What was being-- what was laughing all about? I didn't know. We went outside. Everybody was kissing us. The landlord came over, kissing us. All the neighbors from the whole street was kissing us. What is this? My god, they knew we were Jewish. This whole time, they knew we were Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody, nobody pointed upstairs. That was just all they had to do was to point a finger up there. Jews up there. Take them. They didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were kind souls. These were wonderful people. And we're not talking about one or two. These were a whole street that knew and didn't say one word to those soldiers across the street. What would it have taken for them to tell somebody? Those soldiers were downstairs in that cafe nightly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I couldn't believe it. I was allowed to play. I was allowed to do whatever I wanted. But I still had my own barriers. I thought, well, I can't do this, and I can't do that. Of course you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end of the war, I was eight. I was just-- I was-- yes. I was going to be eight, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your health?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother knew. Just at the end of the war, she looked at me many times and said to my dad, she's not well. I did not look good. And after the war, a lot of the Jewish children were interviewed, were examined and interviewed. And I guess the government was well aware that there had to be problems because of nutritional deficiencies. It was inevitable that people were ill from lack of food and the-- you know, these were the formulating, the formative years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to a lady doctor. My mother took me to a lady doctor. I thought everything was wonderful. But she announced to my parents that I was very ill. I was on the verge of TB.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a term, terminology for this. I do not remember what it is. But it is one step away from TB. And it is caused by lack of nutrition. Although I had food, it wasn't the proper food that a child needs to develop.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the regime was very strict. Once again, I felt I'm in prison again. Do not play. Do not-- in those days, you were not allowed-- if you were ill, you were not allowed to do anything. You had to go to bed. Lie down, calm.

speaker: interviewee
text: She recommended calm. She recommended a stay in the country. She recommended lots of very, very healthy, nutritional foods. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, just-- I, I was gorged with Cream of Wheat and all the best vitamins.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to that place? How did you get to where you needed to be? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents rented an apartment in the outskirts of Brussels, in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they get money to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that they somehow had something saved. There wasn't much. But whatever they had, they managed to do this. They had to. There was no choice. I needed fresh air. I had not had any air, fresh air in years. Maybe just occasionally was not enough for me. And it's amazing, but you do need that. And if the body doesn't get it, it's-- you know, you can become ill for lack of it and the food in combination. So there I was. I remember being in a lawn, on a lawn chair doing nothing, just feeding, feedings. I ran a low-grade temperature for about a year. It just would not leave me. And I kept visiting, going to the doctor every month. I did improve. The lungs were beginning to scar, which is a good sign. Healing process was beginning. And in the meantime, in actuality, my parents were supposed to come to the United States in 1939, 10 years prior. And of course, when the war broke out, all ships-- everything was frozen. Ships were stopped. Hitler did not allow anyone to go through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were basically stuck. And that's the reason for this story. We, we couldn't go. We had visas, everything. So now my parents attempted, once again, to try to emigrate to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the immigration didn't like the looks of my lungs. They just did not-- he said, no. We cannot allow you to pass through. Your daughter is not in good health. But the doctor had released me to go to school, to begin to take on a normal life. My parents kept going, and they said, we'll come back, come back, come back. Well, they made a big issue of the scar tissue there. With letters from the doctor, nothing helped for a long time. In the meantime, my sister was born. And that caused more difficulty, because she needed papers. And so they waited and waited, which gave me a chance to heal and go to school. I think I had about a year and a half of school, that's all. And finally, they agreed. Immigration agreed to let me through with many more letters from the doctor assuring them that I was good enough and capable of going to the United States. And so in 1949, we took off. The HIAS organization helped us, H-I-A-S. And my mother's sister, Renee, and brother, Jack, who immigrated to Indianapolis, sponsored us. But of course, we went to-- we first went to Paris and then boarded a ship in Cannes. And then we went-- after Cannes, we went on to-- we boarded-- yeah, we boarded the ship. And it was a grueling-- it was just a terrible trip. I was very, very ill, my mother and I. Seasickness is a-- ships at that time were not stabilized the way they are today. And we ended up in New York, I believe in Manhattan at the HIAS organization. And they had lodging and food for us. It was a huge building. And they housed many, many families there, and very kind, very wonderful people who do this thing voluntarily for-- who did this thing voluntarily for immigrants. And we were there for a while. And then my parents said well, we've got to do something. I mean, it was a very dismal existence, you know. There was-- we were in a basement. And there was a stove there for I don't know how many people. And I was in a crib somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in a crib?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- they put me in a crib, yeah, because that's all they had. I remember a crib. I remember the bars. And HIAS organization asked me to do them a favor, if I would come to this convention that they were having, a HIAS convention. And would I talk to a group of people-- it turned out to be hundreds-- and talk to Senator Lehman at the time and tell him how much appreciation you had for being brought over by the family and, you know, to be brought over with the family by this HIAS organization and-- just all in French and unrehearsed. I remember being very, very nervous. And I thanked Senator Lehman for his part and all the wonderful people. There must have been 300 people there. I don't know. But they were surrounding me. And here was this little girl of 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had never spoken in public. I'd been so shielded and protected, you know? How was I going to speak? But I did that. I, I acknowledged, you know, how grateful I was for what they did for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my father tried to get a welding job in New York. He was not, he was not successful at it. They tried to get an apartment, and they came upon a terrible situation. They had put down $200 on an apartment. They went to rent-- I still remember the apartment. I was, I was so thrilled. It was, it was really lovely. Landlord, supposed landlord, came to greet us and said that here's this apartment. It's a lovely apartment. But we need $200 down. In 1949, $200 was a lot of money. And my parents, being green and being unfamiliar with the culture-- culture shock, they certainly had-- gave this lady $200 for this apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, my parents proceeded to move into this place. This man came over and said, what are you doing? And he said, well, I'm moving in to my apartment. I paid for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you didn't. You didn't take the apartment from me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the entrance to the United States for my parents. My parents reported her. But she didn't have any money, and it was to no avail. They never, they never got their money back at all. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they stay in that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't even go in. They couldn't, you know. They had nowhere to go. So my family, my Aunt Renee and Uncle Jack and his wife, [? Laney, ?] we inform them of this whole situation of the fraud. And they said, get away from New York. New York is not for you. Come to Indianapolis. We'll help you. We'll do whatever we can to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had been going to school in New York for three months, and I learned English. It was very strange. I was pretty advanced. Not bragging, but the educational system in Europe is a few years ahead. I was advanced, but not knowing the language very well, it was difficult to relate to the teachers. I knew what they were doing but couldn't speak properly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we moved to Indianapolis, so another culture shock. Because that was different. That was small town-- small town and a lot of anti-Semitism. So here I was again, you know. Got it again. Had to kind of keep quiet. Don't tell anybody you're Jewish. Don't mention it in school, because, you know, you don't want them to know. My parents had difficulty getting jobs. They don't speak English. They were refugees, and they were Jewish. So we moved to a basement apartment, which was like a prison. There was no bedroom. It was one big kitchen with a drain in the middle for the washing machine. But it was made into an apartment. And first of all, before that, we moved into a house. A, a lady owned this house. She was a widow. And it was rat-infested. We had one room for the four of us, my sister, Vivienne, and Mom and Dad. And it was just rat-infested. All night long, I would hear the rats and the mice going at each other. It was just-- it was horrible. And to this day, I cannot stand those animals because of it. But this lady did not clean her home. And it was just-- it was a devastating situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we finally-- we moved somewhere else, which was not much better and then ended up in this basement apartment. And like I said, it was a big kitchen-- kitchen, I don't know-- cabinets, a basement, and one other room. And I slept in a hallway. And there were stairs next to me. There was a bed that opened up, and that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Each morning, I had to close the bed. I had nowhere to be, you know. I was very ashamed, very ashamed of this place.

speaker: interviewee
text: People wanted to be friends with me, although I was a real reject because of being from Europe. In 1949 and in early '50s, when you were from Europe, you were not anybody. You were just-- who is this? What is she? Ew, she's from Europe, you know. Real, utmost rejection. So there was again, prison, rejection, you know, deja vu. It, it just went again. And in the meantime, my parents-- I, I can't blame them. They did everything they could, but there was a lot of tension because they had their own worries. They didn't have time for me. They couldn't have. They had to make a living. What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, golly. They wrapped things. I don't know. I think they wrapped salamis in the basement. I don't know. I helped them. I remember plastic bags and wrapping. I, I hardly remember. I think I don't want to. And it was whatever they could find. My mother got a position doing alterations with the Block Company, which was a department store in Indiana, Indianapolis. And she did that. And my father was hired cleaning. He was-- it was pretty demeaning

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop here?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
